metadata.subject: English Language Arts
metadata.description: Creative Writing 12 is the capstone creative writing course, using an advanced writer's workshop model. Students work at near-professional level — developing a portfolio of polished work, writing for external publication and performance, and deepening their understanding of craft and voice. The course challenges students to take artistic risks, engage with diverse literary traditions including First Peoples creative writing, and develop the habits of mind and practice of a committed creative writer.
metadata.general_description: An advanced writer's workshop course developing students as creative writers at near-professional level, with emphasis on portfolio development, writing for authentic audiences, and sophisticated craft across fiction, poetry, creative non-fiction, and script.

big_ideas.statement: The exploration of text and story deepens our understanding of diverse, complex ideas about identity, others, and the world.
big_ideas.elaboration: Senior creative writing engages with the most complex human questions — and the best creative writing opens doors into experiences, identities, and perspectives that would otherwise remain inaccessible.

big_ideas.statement: Texts are socially, culturally, geographically, and historically constructed.
big_ideas.elaboration: Advanced creative writers are aware of the literary traditions they are working within and against — and make conscious choices about how to position their work within a larger cultural conversation.

big_ideas.statement: Language shapes ideas and influences others.
big_ideas.elaboration: At Grade 12, creative writers understand that language is not just the vehicle of meaning but its substance — every choice of word, image, and rhythm is a creative act.

big_ideas.statement: Creative writers take risks and persevere.
big_ideas.elaboration: The most significant creative growth happens at the edge of what feels comfortable — and mature writers have developed the resilience to push there and keep going.

big_ideas.statement: Creative writers are observant of the world.
big_ideas.elaboration: The writer's perception is never idle — the world is always providing raw material, and the Grade 12 writer has developed the sensitivity to notice, capture, and transform it.

big_ideas.statement: Writers write for authentic audiences and real-world purposes.
big_ideas.elaboration: Grade 12 creative writers write for the world — for publication, performance, contest, anthology, and audience — not just for classroom assessment.

categories.name: Comprehend and Connect (Reading, Listening, Viewing)
categories.raw: Comprehend and Connect — Reading, Listening, Viewing

competencies.raw: Read for enjoyment and personal goals; understand and appreciate First Peoples perspectives, diversity, and land/place influence.
i_can_statements: I can read challenging and diverse creative texts for both pleasure and craft analysis.
I can engage with First Peoples creative writing with cultural awareness and genuine literary appreciation.
I can analyze how land and place function as creative themes and formal elements in literature.

competencies.raw: Use information for diverse purposes; evaluate relevance, accuracy, and reliability; understand how forms and features reflect purposes; think critically and creatively to analyze ideas.
i_can_statements: I can conduct research to inform creative work — for setting, character, historical period, or subject matter.
I can analyze mentor texts at a sophisticated craft level, extracting applicable techniques.
I can evaluate my own creative work with the same critical rigor I apply to published writing.

competencies.raw: Identify and understand contexts, values, and perspectives; understand how language constructs identities; construct connections; evaluate literary elements.
i_can_statements: I can analyze how my own identity and positionality shape what I write and how.
I can recognize when my creative work reproduces stereotypes or limited perspectives — and revise accordingly.
I can engage respectfully and authentically with identities and experiences different from my own in my writing.

categories.name: Create and Communicate (Writing, Speaking, Representing)
categories.raw: Create and Communicate — Writing, Speaking, Representing

competencies.raw: Respectfully exchange ideas from diverse perspectives to build shared understanding and transform thinking; respond to text in personal, creative, and critical ways.
i_can_statements: I can contribute substantively to a senior writer's workshop — giving nuanced, specific, useful feedback.
I can receive feedback with openness and use it to make genuine revisions.
I can write responses to published creative work that demonstrate sophisticated craft awareness.

competencies.raw: Select and apply speaking and listening skills in varied contexts; use writing and design processes; express and support opinions with evidence; assess and refine texts; manipulate language purposefully; use figurative and literal language; use writers' practices.
i_can_statements: I can write a craft statement that articulates and defends the artistic choices in a finished piece.
I can manipulate language with precision and intention — using figurative, imagistic, and rhythmic resources purposefully.
I can demonstrate the habits of an active writing practice: regular drafting, revision, and reading.

competencies.raw: Use Canadian conventions proficiently; use acknowledgements and citations; transform ideas to create original texts using varied genres, forms, structures, and styles.
i_can_statements: I can produce a portfolio of polished original creative work in multiple forms.
I can prepare work for submission to journals, contests, or performance with appropriate professional conventions.
I can acknowledge all literary influences, sources, and oral traditions that have shaped my work.

areas.name: Text Forms and Genres (Advanced Reading as a Writer)
areas.raw: Written forms include narrative, journal, procedural, expository, explanatory, news article, e-mail, blog, advertisements, poetry, novel, and letter. Genres include adventure, fable, fairy tale, fantasy, folklore, historical, horror, legend, mystery, mythology, picture book, science fiction, biography, essay, journalism, manual, memoir, personal narrative, speech.
topics: Reading contemporary literary journals: Grain, The Walrus, The New Quarterly, Brick
Advanced fiction: flash fiction, short story, novel excerpt
Advanced poetry: contemporary experimental forms, lyric essay, prose poetry
Advanced creative non-fiction: personal essay, hybrid form, literary journalism
Script: stage play, screen, radio, or podcast drama
First Peoples contemporary creative writing: novels, poetry, drama, essay

areas.name: Text Features and Structures
areas.raw: Form, function, and genre of texts; elements of visual/graphic texts; narrative structures in First Peoples texts; protocols.
topics: Form as meaning: how the shape of a piece embeds its content
Hybrid forms: works that blur genre boundaries
Visual poetry and concrete writing
First Peoples narrative structures as formal resources
Protocols for respectful engagement with First Peoples creative and oral material

areas.name: Strategies and Processes
areas.raw: Reading strategies; oral language strategies; metacognitive strategies; writing processes.
topics: Sustained writing practice: daily/weekly writing habits and a writer's notebook
Advanced workshop: peer and mentor feedback on portfolio work
Submission preparation: cover letters, bios, formatting for journals and contests
Author talks and visiting writers: learning from practitioners
Portfolio curation: selecting, sequencing, and presenting best work
Craft essays and artist statements: reflecting publicly on practice

areas.name: Language Features, Structures, and Conventions
areas.raw: Elements of style; voice exploration (point of view, humour, irony, satire, wit, perspective); usage and conventions; literary elements and devices; literal and inferential meaning.
topics: Voice at the senior level: fully developed, distinctive, and intentional
Image: compression, precision, and the objective correlative
Subtext: what is not said, but felt
Structural choices: openings and endings that do maximum work
Revision as art: the discipline of rethinking, not just correcting
Influence and originality: absorbing what you read and making it new
Irony, ambiguity, and moral complexity in creative writing
Formatting for submission: fonts, margins, headers, line spacing
